mnemonics.title: Quantifier Negation Rules
mnemonics.content:
¬∃x P(x) ≡ ∀x ¬P(x) - 'NOT exists' becomes 'FOR ALL NOT'
¬∀x P(x) ≡ ∃x ¬P(x) - 'NOT for all' becomes 'EXISTS NOT'

mnemonics.title: Inference Rules Quick Reference
mnemonics.content:
UI: ∀x P(x) → P(c) [Universal Instantiation]
EI: ∃x P(x) → P(c) [Existential Instantiation]
MP: P→Q, P ⊢ Q [Modus Ponens]
MT: P→Q, ¬Q ⊢ ¬P [Modus Tollens]

mnemonics.title: Remember FOL Components
mnemonics.content: F O L = Functions + Objects + Logical connectives + Quantifiers + Predicates + Variables

acronyms.usage: Predicate logic with variables and quantifiers, extends propositional logic
Formula structure as conjunction of clauses (each clause is disjunction of literals)
Deriving specific instances from universally quantified statements
Deriving specific instances from existentially quantified statements
The most general substitution making two literals identical during unification
Collection of facts and rules in FOL representing domain knowledge
Goal-driven inference starting from query and working backwards

visualTips.visualization: Draw a circle for domain. ∀ (for all) = fill entire circle. ∃ (exists) = fill at least one point in circle.
Use brackets/underlines to show quantifier scope. Quantifier binds everything within its scope until another quantifier of same variable appears.
∀x (Student(x) → Smart(x)) - draw arrow from Student circle to Smart circle, showing all Student points are inside Smart region.
Build proof tree top-down, matching complementary literals. Empty clause at bottom = contradiction proven.

analogies.analogy: Universal quantifier is like 'ALL' - checks every item in a list. Existential quantifier is like 'SOME' - needs only one match.
Variables are like pronouns in English (he, she, it) - they refer to specific objects in the domain.
Predicates are like verbs and adjectives - they describe properties or relationships between objects.
Inference rules are like recipes - they take known ingredients (premises) and produce new dishes (conclusions) following specific steps.
KB is like an encyclopedia - contains facts (specific statements) and rules (general principles) about a domain.
Unification is like pattern matching - finding values that make two patterns identical, like solving x + 2 = 5 where x = 3.
